skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for designing, implementing, testing, and evaluating MCP servers for external APIs using TypeScript or Python patterns.
skill.summary: Build MCP servers with practical design guidance, implementation patterns, and evaluation workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns. Most are documentation examples or false positives, but the included evaluation harness can launch user-selected MCP servers and let a model call exposed tools. No prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Harness Launches User-Selected Commands
medium_findings.description: The evaluator accepts a stdio command and arguments, then passes them to the MCP stdio client. This is expected for testing local MCP servers, but running untrusted commands can execute arbitrary code on the host.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command path is explicitly supplied by the user and forwarded into StdioServerParameters. The use is legitimate for an MCP evaluator, but the execution risk is real when inputs are untrusted.

medium_findings.title: Model-Driven Tool Calls Lack Destructive-Tool Enforcement
medium_findings.description: The evaluator sends all listed MCP tools to Claude and calls whichever tool the model selects. The guide asks for read-only evaluations, but the script does not enforce readOnlyHint or block destructive tools.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool loop directly executes model-selected tool_use blocks. The documentation recommends read-only questions, but no runtime guard prevents mutation-capable tools from being exposed.

medium_findings.title: Remote Evaluation Can Forward Headers and Tool Data
medium_findings.description: The evaluator supports SSE and HTTP endpoints with user-provided headers. This is useful for authenticated servers, but tool outputs and prompts are also sent through the Anthropic API during evaluation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and header handling is clear and intentional. The risk depends on user-provided endpoints and whether evaluated tools return sensitive data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static Command Findings
low_findings.description: Most shell command detections are fenced documentation examples for builds, inspectors, and evaluator usage. They are not executed automatically by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples and setup instructions. No evidence found that the skill automatically runs these commands outside the optional evaluator.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto alerts appear to match words inside MCP documentation, markdown, or tool annotation examples. No implemented cryptographic algorithm or hashing routine was found in the reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations discuss descriptions, destructive hints, and quality checklists rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar runtime use.

low_findings.title: Example Secrets Are Placeholders, Not Embedded Credentials
low_findings.description: The credential detections are placeholder examples such as API_KEY, bearer tokens, and GitHub token formats. They document how users pass their own credentials during evaluation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The strings are generic placeholders in setup examples, not live credentials. The remaining risk is user handling of real tokens when running the evaluator.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Stdio Server Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluator permits a caller to choose the local MCP server command and arguments. Users should only run trusted server code in this mode.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is intentionally user-controlled and forwarded to the MCP stdio transport. This is a clear execution boundary rather than hidden malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unfiltered Model Tool Invocation Loop
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluator executes model-selected tool calls without a built-in allowlist or read-only filter. Evaluation files and connected servers must be trusted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop directly calls connection.call_tool for selected tools. The risk is moderated because this is an evaluation harness, not hidden background behavior.

content.value_statement: Building MCP servers requires clear tool design, safe transports, and realistic evaluations. This skill provides practical patterns for TypeScript and Python implementations.
actual_capabilities: Explains MCP server planning, tool naming, transport selection, and response design.
Provides TypeScript and Python implementation guidance through reference files.
Covers input schemas, output schemas, pagination, errors, and tool annotations.
Includes an evaluation guide for creating realistic read-only test questions.
Provides a Python evaluation harness for stdio, SSE, and HTTP MCP servers.
Points users to current MCP protocol and SDK documentation sources.
limitations: It is guidance and examples, not a complete generated MCP server.
The evaluator requires trusted MCP servers and careful credential handling.
Reference examples use placeholder APIs and must be adapted before production use.
It does not enforce read-only tool execution during evaluation runs.

use_cases.title: Design a New MCP Integration
use_cases.description: Plan tool names, schemas, transport, authentication, pagination, and response formats before implementation.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing MCP Server
use_cases.description: Review tool descriptions, annotations, errors, and workflow coverage against practical MCP best practices.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Agent Usability
use_cases.description: Create read-only evaluation questions and run them against a trusted MCP server.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My MCP Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a single API integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to plan an MCP server for my API. Recommend tools, names, schemas, transport, and evaluation questions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Tool Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of an existing tool list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to review these MCP tools. Check naming, descriptions, inputs, outputs, annotations, pagination, and errors.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Core Patterns
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced implementation support for TypeScript or Python.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to help implement a production MCP server. Follow the TypeScript or Python reference patterns and explain tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Create Evaluation Suite
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced validation of an MCP server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to create ten read-only evaluation questions. Make answers stable, verifiable, and realistic for agent testing.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output_examples.output: A tool inventory with naming conventions, schema recommendations, pagination rules, transport choice, and a testing checklist.

output_examples.input: Review these MCP tool descriptions for agent usability.
output_examples.output: A prioritized review covering unclear names, missing parameters, weak errors, risky annotations, and suggested replacements.

output_examples.input: Create evaluation questions for a read-only GitHub MCP server.
output_examples.output: A set of realistic questions with stable expected answers and notes for manual verification.

best_practices: Use trusted local commands and trusted remote endpoints when running the evaluator.
Keep evaluation tasks read-only and separate them from destructive production tools.
Adapt reference examples to your service security model before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not expose broad destructive tools to the evaluator without an allowlist.
Do not paste real credentials into shared evaluation files or reports.
Do not copy placeholder API examples into production without validation and rate limits.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design, implement, test, and evaluate MCP servers for external APIs and services.

faq.question: Does it support TypeScript and Python?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for TypeScript MCP SDK patterns and Python FastMCP patterns.

faq.question: Does it create a complete server automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides structure, examples, and review guidance that you adapt to your service.

faq.question: Is the included evaluator safe for any server?
faq.answer: No. Run it only against trusted servers because it can invoke tools selected by a model.

faq.question: Can I use it for remote MCP servers?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance covers streamable HTTP, and the evaluator supports HTTP and SSE endpoints.

faq.question: How should I handle credentials?
faq.answer: Use environment variables or secure runtime configuration. Avoid storing real tokens in prompts, examples, or reports.
